transcript:
[00:00:01] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Hey Marcus, can you hear me okay?

[00:00:03] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, yeah, loud and clear. Can you see me or is my camera — oh wait, there we go. Sorry, Zoom was being weird for a second.

[00:00:09] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): No worries at all, happens to me like five times a day. Um, so thanks for making time today. I know you mentioned on our email thread that things are pretty hectic over there right now.

[00:00:18] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, I mean, that's an understatement. We're in the middle of three commercial builds right now, we're hiring like crazy, and then on top of all that I'm trying to figure out this whole website situation. So yeah. Hectic is one word for it. [laughs]

[00:00:31] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): [laughs] Well I appreciate you carving out the time. So I figured we could use this call to really understand what's going on with your current site, where you want to go, and then I can share some thoughts on how we might be able to help. And if it's not a fit, that's totally fine too — I'll tell you that honestly. Sound good?

[00:00:47] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, sounds great. I like that. No hard sell. I've had, uh, I've talked to a couple other agencies this month and some of them just launch right into their pitch deck and I'm like, buddy, you don't even know what I need yet.

[00:00:59] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Oh yeah, I hate that. [laughs] We're not going to do that. So, okay, let's start at the beginning. Tell me about NovaBuild. I did some research — you guys do commercial construction, right? But give me the version you'd tell someone at a bar.

[00:01:14] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): [laughs] The bar version. Okay so, NovaBuild — we've been around for about twelve years. We build commercial properties. So think office buildings, retail centers, medical facilities, that kind of thing. We're based in Denver but we operate across Colorado and we've started expanding into Utah and Arizona in the last couple years. Um, about 300 employees, give or take, depending on the season.

[00:01:38] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Okay, nice. And how's business overall?

[00:01:41] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Business is, honestly, really good. Like, the pipeline is strong, we're winning more bids than we're losing, which is great. But here's the thing — and this is kind of why we're talking — we're winning in spite of our online presence, not because of it. Like, we're winning because our sales team is incredible and because we have a really solid reputation in the Denver market. But every time someone Googles us before a meeting, I just... I cringe.

[00:02:07] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Yeah. So tell me about the current website. What's the situation there?

[00:02:12] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Oh god, where do I start. So, the current site was built about five years ago. We hired this freelance developer — nice guy, really — his name was Derek. And Derek built us a WordPress site, it was fine at the time, you know, it looked decent, it had our projects on it, whatever. And then Derek just... vanished. Like, I'm not even being dramatic. One day his email bounced, his phone was disconnected, his LinkedIn went dark. I still don't know what happened to the guy. Like, I hope he's okay honestly but—

[00:02:44] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): [laughs] Oh no, that's — I mean, I know it's not funny, but we hear this story so often you wouldn't believe it. The disappearing freelancer is like a rite of passage for companies.

[00:02:54] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): [laughs] Yeah, right? So after Derek ghosted us, we were just kind of stuck. Like, nobody on our team really knows WordPress. Our, uh, our marketing coordinator Sarah, she can update blog posts and swap out photos, but anything beyond that, we're lost. And over the years things have just... degraded. Plugins are outdated, the theme hasn't been updated, I think we're still running some version of PHP that's probably a security risk. I don't know.

[00:03:21] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): And how does the site look and feel right now? Like, if I pulled it up—

[00:03:25] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): I mean, you can pull it up right now if you want. Actually, you know what, don't. [laughs] I'm embarrassed. It looks like a website from 2019, because it literally is. The hero image is of a building we completed four years ago. The mobile experience is awful — like, you have to pinch and zoom to read anything on a phone. It loads slow, especially the portfolio page because all the images are huge and uncompressed. And the design is just... it's very template-y. It doesn't reflect who we are now. We were a 150-person company when that site was built. We've doubled since then.

[00:03:59] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Yeah. So it sounds like the site hasn't kept pace with where the company actually is.

[00:04:04] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Exactly. That's exactly it. And look, I could live with a dated-looking site if it wasn't actually costing us business. But Rachel, I gotta tell you what happened about two months ago because this is really the thing that lit a fire under all of this.

[00:04:18] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Yeah, tell me.

[00:04:19] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): So we were bidding on a project — a medical office complex in Aurora, pretty big deal, about a two million dollar contract. We made it to the final two. Our proposal was strong, our pricing was competitive, we had great references. And we lost it. And when our sales director, Karen, followed up to ask for feedback, the prospect literally said — and I'm quoting here — "honestly, we went to your website and it made us question whether you're still in business."

[00:04:50] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Ouch.

[00:04:51] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah. Ouch is right. Two million dollars. Gone. Because of a website. I mean, I couldn't believe it. Actually, no, I could believe it, that's the worst part. I knew the site was bad. I'd been saying it for a year. But losing an actual contract over it — that got everyone's attention real fast. Including our CEO, Tom. Tom was, uh, Tom was not happy.

[00:05:14] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): I bet. And that kind of story, it's — look, the hard truth is that in commercial construction, the clients you're going after, they're making decisions about who to trust with multi-million-dollar projects. And they're absolutely going to judge you by your digital presence. It's like showing up to a pitch meeting in a wrinkled shirt.

[00:05:31] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): That's — yeah, that's a great analogy actually. Our CEO Tom says something similar. He's like, "we wouldn't show up to a client meeting with a beat-up truck and dirty boots, so why is our website the digital equivalent of that?"

[00:05:43] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Tom sounds like a smart guy. [laughs] So, okay. That's the pain. Let me make sure I understand what you're looking for. Walk me through — in an ideal world, what does the new site do for NovaBuild?

[00:05:56] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Okay, yeah. So I've actually been, um, I've been putting together kind of a wish list. Some of this is stuff I need, some of this is more aspirational. But let me just walk through it. First and foremost, it needs to look premium. Like, when a prospect lands on our site, I want them to go "oh, these guys are legit." Our work is phenomenal — we've built some genuinely impressive buildings — and the site needs to reflect that quality.

[00:06:22] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Totally.

[00:06:23] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Second, we need a real project portfolio. Not just a grid of photos — like, actual case studies. Before and after, specs, client testimonials, timeline, the whole thing. Right now we've got like twelve projects on the site and we've completed probably sixty since then. And the ones that are on there, it's just a photo and a one-line description. It doesn't tell the story at all.

[00:06:47] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Right, and that project portfolio is probably the single most important thing on the site for a construction company. That's what prospects want to see — have you built something like what I need?

[00:06:56] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): A hundred percent. And then, um, lead capture. So right now we have a contact form that goes to a generic email inbox that, honestly, I don't think anyone checks regularly. We use HubSpot for our CRM — we've had it for about two years — and the website doesn't talk to it at all. I want form submissions going straight into HubSpot, I want to be able to track which pages a prospect visited, maybe do some lead scoring. You know, actually treat inbound leads like they matter.

[00:07:24] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Yeah, we do a ton of HubSpot integrations. That's very doable. What tier of HubSpot are you on, do you know?

[00:07:30] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Uh, Professional, I think? Marketing Hub Professional. We're not really using it to its full potential if I'm being honest. But that's a separate problem.

[00:07:39] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): That's actually great because Professional has all the API access we'd need. We can do some really cool stuff with that. But keep going — what else is on the list?

[00:07:47] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Okay so — careers page. This is a big one. We're growing fast, Rachel. We need to hire about forty people this year. Field crews, project managers, estimators, engineers. And right now our careers page is, I kid you not, it's a PDF that you have to download. It's a PDF. [laughs] In 2026.

[00:08:07] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): [laughs] A PDF! Oh no.

[00:08:09] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, our HR director James, James Morton, he's been on my case about this for months. He's like, "Marcus, I can't attract top talent when the careers page looks like a government form from 2005." And he's right. We're competing for skilled tradespeople and PMs with bigger firms that have beautiful career portals with video testimonials from employees and benefits breakdowns and all that stuff.

[00:08:33] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Yeah, recruitment marketing is a huge thing now, especially in construction where the labor market is so tight. We actually built a really robust careers section for another client — I'll tell you about them in a minute — with department filters, video content from current employees, an application tracking integration. It made a big difference for them.

[00:08:51] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): That's exactly the kind of thing we need. And then — okay, this one is more of a phase two thing, but I want to mention it — we've been talking about building a client portal. So basically, once a client signs with us, they'd be able to log in and see their project status. Like, milestones completed, photos from the job site, upcoming deadlines, budget tracking, that kind of thing. Right now we do all of that through email and weekly PDF reports and it's, it's not great.

[00:09:18] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): That's really interesting. Yeah, I think you're right that that's probably a phase two thing — it's a more complex build. But it's great that you're thinking about it because we can architect the phase one work to support that later. You don't want to build the foundation of a house and then realize you need to tear it up to add plumbing, you know?

[00:09:35] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): [laughs] I literally build houses for a living and that analogy still works on me. Yeah, no, that makes sense. Plan for it now, build it later.

[00:09:43] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Exactly. So let me make sure I've got the full picture. The core needs for phase one are: modern redesign that positions you as a premium brand, robust project portfolio with case studies, HubSpot-integrated lead capture, and a careers section that helps you attract and hire forty people. And then phase two is the client portal. Am I missing anything?

[00:10:04] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Um... I think that covers the big stuff. Oh — SEO. I should mention SEO. We do basically zero organic search right now. Like, if you Google "commercial construction Denver," we're nowhere. And I know a new site alone doesn't fix that, but I want the foundation to be there. Like, I want the site to be built in a way that we can actually rank for things.

[00:10:24] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Yeah, absolutely. So the way we think about it is, the new site should be technically optimized from the ground up — fast load times, proper schema markup, clean URL structure, mobile-first, all of that. And then the ongoing SEO strategy — content, backlinks, local SEO — that's something we can either help with on a retainer basis or you can bring in an SEO specialist to work on top of the foundation we build. But the foundation matters a lot.

[00:10:48] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, makes sense.

[00:10:50] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Cool. So can I tell you about a client of ours? Because the parallels are kind of uncanny.

[00:10:56] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, please.

[00:10:57] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): So we worked with a company called Ridgeline Construction out of Portland. Similar size to you guys — about 250 employees at the time, commercial and industrial builds. They came to us in almost the exact same situation. Old WordPress site, couldn't update it themselves, their competitors were looking way sharper online. And their main pain point was also the portfolio — they had all this incredible work and none of it was represented well on their site.

[00:11:22] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, that sounds really familiar.

[00:11:24] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): So what we built for them was — we did a full redesign on Next.js with a headless CMS on the backend. And the CMS piece is important because it means their marketing team can go in and add new projects, update content, publish blog posts, all without touching any code. And for the portfolio specifically, we created this really slick template where they upload the project details and photos, and it generates these beautiful interactive case studies with before/after sliders, aerial drone footage integration, specs, testimonials, the whole nine yards.

[00:11:55] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Wait — before and after sliders? Like, you can drag and see the empty lot versus the finished building?

[00:12:01] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Exactly. You drag the slider across and it goes from the groundbreaking photo to the finished building. Clients love it. It's such a visual way to show the transformation.

[00:12:10] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Okay, that's — I'm getting excited now. Because we have so much drone footage from our job sites. We fly drones for progress reporting anyway, so we've got all this amazing aerial content that's just sitting on a hard drive somewhere. The idea of using that on the site — yeah, that would be incredible.

[00:12:26] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Totally. And I'll tell you what happened for Ridgeline after the site launched. In the first six months, their inbound leads increased by about 40%. Their average time on the portfolio pages was over four minutes, which is really high. And — this is the best part — they told us they won a $5 million contract where the client specifically mentioned the website as a factor in their decision.

[00:12:48] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Okay, so literally the opposite of what happened to us. Instead of losing a deal because of the site, they won one because of it.

[00:12:55] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Exactly. And that's — look, I can't guarantee specific results, every situation is different. But the point is, in your industry, the website is a trust signal. Especially for a company your size going after the contracts you're going after. It matters.

[00:13:08] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah. No, I agree. I mean, that's the whole reason we're having this conversation. Okay so — let me ask you about technology because our CTO, Priya Sharma, is going to ask me about this. You mentioned Next.js. Um, we looked at, like, Squarespace and Wix initially, just because they seemed simple. But I talked to Priya and she was like, "Marcus, we're a 300-person company, we're not going to run our website on Squarespace." [laughs]

[00:13:36] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): [laughs] Priya's not wrong. I mean, there's nothing inherently bad about those platforms, but they have limitations, especially when you're talking about custom integrations like HubSpot, a client portal down the road, complex portfolio functionality — you'd hit a ceiling really fast. Plus, from a performance standpoint, you'd be limited in how much you can optimize.

[00:13:56] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, that was kind of our conclusion too. Like, it felt too "small business" for where we are. But then the question becomes, okay, what do we build on? And honestly, I don't know enough about web technology to evaluate that myself.

[00:14:09] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): So let me break it down in non-technical terms because I think this is important. What we'd recommend for a project like this is what's called a headless CMS architecture. Basically, the content management system — where your team goes to update pages, add projects, post jobs — that's separate from the front-end, which is what visitors see. The front-end we'd build in Next.js, which is a React framework. It's what most modern high-performance websites are built on. The benefits are speed — like, really fast page loads — great SEO out of the box, and it's super flexible for building custom features.

[00:14:44] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Okay. And the headless CMS — is that something our marketing coordinator Sarah would be able to use? Because she's not technical at all. She can use WordPress, she's comfortable with that, but...

[00:14:55] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Absolutely. So the CMS we'd likely recommend is either Sanity or Contentful — both are very user-friendly. Think of it like... you know how Google Docs is way simpler than Microsoft Word? It's kind of like that. The interface is clean, it's intuitive, Sarah would be able to manage content, add portfolio projects, post jobs, all through a nice dashboard. We'd do training with her as part of the project.

[00:15:18] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Okay, cool. Priya's going to want to dig into this more, I'm sure. She'll probably want to talk to your technical team directly.

[00:15:24] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Absolutely, we can set that up. We have a solutions architect, David, who would be on this project, and he's great at talking through the technical details. We can schedule a follow-up call specifically for Priya if she'd like.

[00:15:35] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, that'd be great. She'd appreciate that.

[00:15:38] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): So, um, let me ask you about timeline. You mentioned in your email that you have some kind of event coming up?

[00:15:45] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, so — this is kind of the forcing function. We're sponsoring a booth at BuildCon this year. It's the big industry conference, happens in July. July 18th through the 20th, I think. And we're not just attending, we're a Gold sponsor, so our brand is going to be everywhere. Big booth, speaking slot, the whole thing. And our CEO Tom is really, really insistent that the new website is live before BuildCon. Because we're going to be handing out cards, driving people to the site, all of that. He does not want people going to the current site.

[00:16:18] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Okay, so July 18th. That gives us, what, about four months from today?

[00:16:24] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Roughly. Yeah. Is that... is that realistic? Because I genuinely don't know.

[00:16:29] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): I'm going to be honest with you. It's tight. It's not impossible, but it's tight, especially with the scope you're describing. Let me walk through what a typical timeline looks like for a project like this. Usually we do about two to three weeks of discovery and strategy — that's where we really nail down the sitemap, the user journeys, the content strategy. Then design is usually four to six weeks. Development is another six to eight weeks. And then you've got content migration, testing, QA, that's another two to three weeks. So all in, you're looking at roughly fourteen to twenty weeks, and four months is about seventeen weeks.

[00:17:07] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): So it could work, but there's not a lot of room for, uh, for slippage.

[00:17:12] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Right. And the biggest risk factor honestly isn't usually on our side — it's content. Like, we can design and build on schedule, but if we're waiting three weeks for portfolio photos to get selected and testimonials to get approved and bio copy to get written, that's where projects get delayed. So we'd need a real commitment from your team to turn things around quickly during the content phase.

[00:17:33] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, that makes sense. And I — look, I know our team. We're all busy. But I think the BuildCon deadline actually helps because it gives everyone a real reason to prioritize this. Like, it's not just "whenever we get to it." There's a hard date.

[00:17:47] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Deadlines are motivating, for sure. One thing we could also talk about is a phased launch. So instead of trying to get everything perfect by July 18th, we could prioritize the core pages — homepage, about, portfolio, contact — and get those live and looking amazing for BuildCon. And then things like the full careers section build-out, some of the deeper case studies, blog migration — that could roll in over the weeks after launch. That way you've got a strong site for the conference, and it continues to get better over time.

[00:18:16] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Hmm. I like that actually. Because trying to have literally everything perfect on day one — yeah, I could see that being stressful. As long as the core site looks great for the conference, we can handle the careers section being a little more basic initially and then beefing it up. James might push back but I think I can manage him.

[00:18:35] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): And for what it's worth, we could still have a functional careers page for launch — it just might be the version one rather than the fully tricked-out version with video testimonials and department filters and all that.

[00:18:45] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, that's fair. Okay. So timeline is doable but we need to be disciplined about it. Got it.

[00:18:52] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Exactly. And we'd have a dedicated project manager keeping everyone on track. Weekly status calls, clear deadlines for feedback rounds, all of that. We've done enough of these to know where projects get stuck, so we proactively manage those risk points.

[00:19:05] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Good, good. Okay so — and I know this is the part everyone hates talking about, but — budget. Let me just tell you where my head's at and you can tell me if I'm way off base. So I did some research, talked to a few people, and I was thinking somewhere in the $30,000 to $50,000 range for the website project. But honestly I pulled those numbers kind of out of thin air, so...

[00:19:30] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Okay. So, first of all, you're not wildly off base, which is good. But let me give you some more nuanced context. For a project of this scope — and I'm talking about the full phase one with the custom portfolio system, HubSpot integration, careers section, premium design, responsive build — you're probably looking at more like the $60,000 to $85,000 range. Now, I know that's higher than what you mentioned, so let me explain what's driving that.

[00:19:57] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Okay. Yeah. That's — um, that's more than I expected. Go ahead.

[00:20:02] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): So the design alone — for a premium brand experience with custom illustrations, professional photography art direction, interactive elements like those portfolio sliders we talked about — that's a significant investment. You don't get a Squarespace look at a Squarespace price, you know? And then the development side, the HubSpot integration, the CMS setup, the portfolio system, the careers backend — these are custom-built features, not just installing a plugin. And the reason that matters is that custom-built features work exactly the way you want them to, they perform better, and they scale as your business grows.

[00:20:36] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Right. No, I — I hear you. And I'm not saying it's unreasonable, I just need to, I need to think about how I frame this internally. Because Tom, our CEO, he knows we need a new site, but I think in his head he's thinking like $20K. [laughs] He's a construction guy, he thinks in terms of materials and labor, and the idea of spending $70K on a website might give him a heart attack.

[00:20:58] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): [laughs] I hear you. And actually, I think there's a really compelling way to frame this for Tom. That contract you lost — $2 million, right?

[00:21:06] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah.

[00:21:07] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): So, the margin on a $2 million commercial construction contract, what's that typically look like?

[00:21:13] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Uh, it varies, but probably 12 to 15 percent on that type of project. So, $240K to $300K in profit, roughly.

[00:21:21] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Right. So you left $300K in profit on the table because of a bad website. The new website pays for itself if it helps you win one contract like that. And it's not just about winning new business — it's about not losing business. How many prospects visited your site last year and quietly decided not to reach out because it didn't inspire confidence? You'll never even know that number.

[00:21:42] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah. No, that's — that's a really good point. I literally just got chills because you're right. The two million dollar contract is the one we know about because they told us. But how many people Googled us, went to the site, and just... moved on? That thought keeps me up at night honestly.

[00:21:58] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): And that's the ROI conversation to have with Tom. This isn't an expense — it's an investment in your company's most visible asset. You know, I had another client tell me once, "our website works 24/7, it never takes a sick day, and it talks to more prospects than our entire sales team combined." Which is kind of true, right?

[00:22:16] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): [laughs] Yeah, I like that. Okay so — let me ask this. Is there any flexibility in the pricing? Like, could we scope it differently to bring it closer to our original number? Or is $60K really the floor?

[00:22:28] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): There's definitely flexibility depending on scope. Like, we could simplify the portfolio system, or use more templated designs versus fully custom, or reduce the number of interior pages in the initial build. We could probably get it down to the $50K to $60K range with some smart scoping. What I wouldn't want to do is cut corners in a way that means you end up back here in two years needing another rebuild. The goal is to build something that grows with you for the next five-plus years.

[00:22:55] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): No, I agree. Like, we did the cheap route last time and look where it got us. I'd rather invest properly and do it right. I just need to get the budget approved. Um — you know what would actually be really helpful? If you could put together some kind of proposal or summary that I could present to Tom and Priya. Like, something that shows what we'd get, what the investment is, and the ROI argument. Because I'm basically going to have to sell this internally.

[00:23:21] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): A hundred percent. We do this all the time. We can put together what we call a "business case" document — it's not a full proposal yet, because we'd need to do more discovery to scope it properly — but it's a summary of the problem, the recommended solution, timeline, investment range, and the ROI case. And if it would help, I'm happy to jump on a call with Tom and Priya directly. Sometimes hearing it from the agency helps because we can answer their questions in real time.

[00:23:46] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Yeah, actually, that would be huge. If you could present it with me. Because Tom's going to ask questions I might not be able to answer, especially on the technical side. And Priya will definitely want to grill someone on the architecture.

[00:23:58] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Let's do it. I'll loop in David, our solutions architect, for Priya. We can do a 45-minute presentation. How soon could you get everyone in a room — or on a Zoom?

[00:24:08] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Um, let me think. Tom is traveling this week but he's back Monday. Priya is pretty flexible. Maybe next Wednesday or Thursday? I could probably also get James on that call too, the HR director, since the careers piece is so important to him.

[00:24:23] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): That would be great, actually. The more stakeholders we can get aligned early, the smoother the project goes. Nothing worse than getting halfway through design and having someone say, "wait, I never agreed to this direction."

[00:24:34] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): [laughs] Oh god, yeah, I've been through that. Okay, I'll get it on the calendar and send you an invite.

[00:24:40] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Perfect. Now, I want to circle back to something you mentioned earlier that I think is important. You said a competitor — Meridian, was it? — just launched a new site?

[00:24:49] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild Construction): Meridian Builders, yeah. And it's, ugh, it's nice. Like, really nice. They did a full rebrand and the new website is sharp. Great photography, this cool interactive project map showing all their builds across the state, video content, the whole thing. And people in the industry have noticed. I've had two different people at networking events say to me, "have you seen Meridian's new site?" And I'm standing there like, "yeah, it's great," while dying inside because our site looks like it was built on GeoCities in comparison.

[00:25:22] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): [laughs] Not quite GeoCities, but I hear you. And here's the thing — that interactive project map they have? We can do that.
